source: British Council–EAQUALS Core Inventory for General English, 2nd ed. 2015

strands.title: Rhetoric, argument & persuasion at the highest level
Mastery in professional, academic & public contexts

cards.title: Precise vocabulary for exact shades of meaning
cards.goal: Can select the exact word or phrase to convey a fine distinction of meaning, rejecting near-synonyms that don't quite fit.
cards.evidence: Given a set of near-synonyms, choose and justify the exact word for five different contexts, explaining what would be lost or changed with each alternative.

cards.title: Editing and refining own language
cards.goal: Can revise his/her own spoken or written output on the fly to improve precision, register or effect.
discourse: real-time revision: or, more accurately…, let me rephrase that more carefully…
cards.evidence: Deliver a short unscripted statement, then immediately revise it aloud for greater precision or better effect, articulating exactly what changed and why.
skin_examples: refining a strong claim after saying it
improving a description in the moment

cards.title: Full command of tense and aspect
cards.goal: Can deploy the entire tense/aspect system flexibly and without conscious effort, including rare combinations, to serve meaning rather than habit.
grammar: full tense/aspect system used flexibly and idiomatically, including rare combinations (e.g. future perfect continuous, past subjunctive)
cards.evidence: Narrate an extended, unrehearsed account (4+ minutes) with no perceptible strain or self-correction around tense or aspect, verified by the listener being unable to identify a single error.
skin_examples: an extended personal or professional account
a complex hypothetical scenario told as a story

cards.title: Defending a sophisticated argument under sustained challenge
cards.goal: Can maintain and adapt a complex argument through sustained, genuine challenge without losing coherence or composure.
cards.evidence: Defend a position through five consecutive rounds of genuine, escalating challenge from the teacher, adapting the argument each time while remaining consistent with the overall thesis.

cards.title: Deliberate rhetorical effect
cards.goal: Can choose and combine rhetorical devices deliberately for a specific emotional or persuasive effect.
grammar: irony, understatement, hyperbole and rhetorical questions combined deliberately
cards.evidence: Deliver a two-minute persuasive or emotive piece combining at least three distinct rhetorical devices, then explain the intended effect of each choice.
skin_examples: a speech meant to move an audience

cards.title: Reframing an argument for a different audience
cards.goal: Can take the same underlying argument and reframe its register, emphasis and examples for a markedly different audience, in real time.
cards.evidence: Present the same core argument twice back-to-back for two very different audiences (e.g. experts vs laypeople, formal vs casual), with the teacher confirming both are fully appropriate to their audience.
skin_examples: explaining a decision to a board vs to a friend
pitching the same idea formally and informally
adapting a message for two different audiences

cards.title: Synthesising and critically evaluating multiple complex sources
cards.goal: Can combine several complex, possibly conflicting sources into a coherent account and evaluate their relative reliability or strength.
cards.evidence: Given three complex conflicting sources, deliver a critical synthesis that not only reports each position but evaluates which is best supported and why.

cards.title: Mediating between positions
cards.goal: Can accurately reformulate each side of a disagreement for the other, facilitating mutual understanding without distorting either position.
discourse: mediation language: what I think you're both saying is…, X, I think Y's real concern is…, does that fairly represent your view?
cards.evidence: Mediate a simulated disagreement between two roleplay positions, accurately restating each side to the other's satisfaction and identifying the actual point of difference.
skin_examples: translating between two stances in a debate

cards.title: Summarising complex input with full retention of nuance
cards.goal: Can summarise long or complex spoken or written input, preserving not just content but tone and emphasis.
cards.evidence: After a long, nuanced input (a talk, an article, a complex explanation), produce a 60-second summary that a listener familiar with the original confirms captures both the content and the original tone.

cards.title: Idiom, wordplay and humour
cards.goal: Can use and understand idiom, wordplay and humour appropriately, including recognising when a literal reading is not intended.
lexis: idiom and wordplay: puns, double meanings, culturally-loaded idiom
cards.evidence: Correctly interpret the intended humour in five idiomatic or wordplay-based examples, then produce one original piece of wordplay or a well-placed idiom that lands as intended.
skin_examples: explaining a joke that relies on wordplay
using an idiom at exactly the right moment

cards.title: Implicit cultural and contextual reference
cards.goal: Can recognise and appropriately respond to reference that depends on shared cultural or contextual knowledge rather than explicit statement.
cards.evidence: Given five utterances that rely on implicit cultural or situational knowledge to be understood, correctly explain what is being implied and respond appropriately to at least three.
skin_examples: a culturally loaded reference in conversation
an allusion the speaker expects to be understood

cards.title: Natural colloquial register
cards.goal: Can produce casual, colloquial speech that is indistinguishable in naturalness from a proficient native speaker in relaxed contexts.
grammar: natural ellipsis, contraction and informal syntax in casual speech
lexis: current colloquial expressions appropriate to the student's social context
cards.evidence: Sustain 5 minutes of genuinely relaxed, unplanned conversation with no register slips into overly formal or textbook phrasing, confirmed by the teacher.

cards.title: Extended unscripted talk with audience adaptation
cards.goal: Can deliver a long, fully fluent, unscripted talk, adapting on the fly to visible audience reaction.
cards.evidence: Deliver a 6+ minute unscripted talk on a substantial topic, adjusting pace, depth or direction at least once in response to a visible signal (a question, a confused look, a comment) from the listener.

cards.title: Handling hostile, ambiguous or high-stakes exchanges
cards.goal: Can remain fully in control of language and composure in a difficult, ambiguous or high-pressure exchange.
cards.evidence: Handle a deliberately hostile or ambiguous roleplay (a difficult interview, an adversarial exchange) for five minutes with full linguistic control and no visible loss of composure.

cards.title: Producing and critiquing formal register orally
cards.goal: Can accurately paraphrase and critique a piece of dense, formal written text (legal, academic, official) on the spot in spoken form.
lexis: formal/technical register of the student's own field or of official documents
cards.evidence: Given a dense formal text, paraphrase it accurately in plain spoken English within two minutes of reading it, then offer one critical observation about it.

cards.title: Deliberate use of marked and rare structures
cards.goal: Can exploit rare, literary or highly formal structures deliberately for effect, understanding exactly what register they signal.
grammar: literary/formal inversion, archaic or highly formal forms, deliberate marked word order
cards.evidence: Produce three sentences using rare or marked structures in contexts where the choice is clearly deliberate and appropriate, then explain what each choice signals about register or tone.
skin_examples: a deliberately literary piece of storytelling
